coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots modified Lu2O3 deep-ultraviolet photodetectors: Uncovering the potential for high-efficiency detection with 44.6 % EQE
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dan
Li, Weisen
Liang, Jiarong
Lin, Zhuogeng
Tang, Xingui
Zheng, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a high-performance Lu2O3-based deep-ultraviolet (DUV) photodetector (PD) has been designed by embedding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the defect passivation layer and the fast carrier transport channel at the Pt/Lu2O3 interface. Meanwhile, the enhanced internal electric field provided by GQDs/Lu2O3 leads to an enhanced photovoltaic effect, which is further coupled with the enhanced pyroelectric effect caused by the GQDs. All of these result in the fabricated Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN device achieving a high photoresponsivity (R) of 92.6 mA/W, a high external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 44.6 %, and a large detectivity (D*) of 3.5×1011 Jones at a bias voltage of 0 V and under 258 nm illumination with 0.72 μW light power. Compared to Pt/Lu2O3/GaN devices without embedded GQDs layers, this device has a higher R, a larger D*, and higher EQE. In addition, the application of the device in secure optical communication systems has verified the feasibility of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN in decrypting encrypted optical signals into readable text information. This work provides a new reference for the fabrication of high-performance DUV photovoltaic PDs based on Lu2O3.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Deep-ultraviolet
Photodetector
Photovoltaic
Graphene quantum dots
External quantum efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of constructing and working principles of high EQE DUV photovoltaic PD.

(a) Film preparation and device manufacturing process. (b) Comparison of Lu2O3/GaN XRD pattern with the Lu2O3 standard powder diffraction card (JCPDS Card No. 43-1021). (c) Raman spectra of GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN (blue line) and Lu2O3/GaN (gray line). (d) Dameter distribution of the GQDs adopted. The inset is the TEM image of the GQDs. (e) SEM image of Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction. (f) Transmission spectrum of Lu2O3 film grown on sapphire substrate under the same film synthesis conditions. The inset is the variation of (αhν)2 with photon energy (hν). α is the absorption coefficient of Lu2O3. (g) Transmission spectra of Pt and Pt-GQDs electrode attached on sapphire.

Basic optoelectronic properties of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN device. (a) I-V curves of the device in dark and 258 nm illumination. (b) Variation of R365 nm and R258 nm with varying voltage. (c) I-V curves of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3 contact. The inset is the GaN/In contact I-V curves. (d) Multi period I-T of the devices under period 9.4 μW 258 nm illumination conditions and 0 V bias. (e) I-V curves of the device under varied DUV power irradiation. DUV power depend of VOC (f) and ISC (g). (h) Photo-to-dark current ratio (PDCR) of the device under varied power DUV irradiation

(a) Multi-period I-T curves of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN under 0 V bias and DUV illumination with variable power. Heat transfer process simulation of Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction under 0 s (b), 1.5 s (c) continuous radiating heat source (which is simulated as.the DUV irradiation) with a power density of 6.96 W/m2. Temperature difference between the upper and lower surfaces of Lu2O3 film under 0 ms (b1), 1 μs (c1) continuous DUV irradiation. Direction of Ebuilt-in and Epyro under ΔT=0 (b2), ΔT>0 (c2).

(a) Pyroelectric-photovoltaic coupled effect indueded Iphoto+pyro and photovoltaic effect indueded Iphoto versus optical power, respectivly. The corresponding reponsivity (b), enhancement factor (c), EQE (d) and detectivity (e) under DUV illumination with different power. (f) Rise and Decay times of device under DUV illumination with different optical power.

Performance comparison between Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN (PD1) and Pt/Lu2O3/GaN (PD2). (a) I-V curves of the devices in dark. (b) I-V curves of the devices in DUV irradiation. (c) I-T curves of the devices at 0 V bias. (d) Rphoto, D*photo of PD1 and PD2. (e) Rphoto, D*photo of PD1 and PD2. (f) Ratio of Rphoto, D*photo, PDCR and EQEphoto between PD1 and PD2. (g) Forward I-V curves of PD1 and PD2 in dark. Calculation results of ideal factor and Schottky barrier height for PD1 (h) and PD2 (i).
(a) Confidential optical communication system. (b) Pulse definition. (c) Decryption results of optical signals.
Performance comparison of the DUV photovoltaic PDs based on wide bandgap semiconductors.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots modified Lu2O3 deep-ultraviolet photodetectors: Uncovering the potential for high-efficiency detection with 44.6 % EQE

School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials, Shenzhen Campus of Sun Yat-sen University, Sun Yat-sen University, Shenzhen 518107, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Campus of Sun Yat-sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Materials, Shenzhen Campus of Sun Yat-sen University, Sun Yat-sen University, Shenzhen 518107, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a high-performance Lu2O3-based deep-ultraviolet (DUV) photodetector (PD) has been designed by embedding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the defect passivation layer and the fast carrier transport channel at the Pt/Lu2O3 interface. Meanwhile, the enhanced internal electric field provided by GQDs/Lu2O3 leads to an enhanced photovoltaic effect, which is further coupled with the enhanced pyroelectric effect caused by the GQDs. All of these result in the fabricated Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN device achieving a high photoresponsivity (R) of 92.6 mA/W, a high external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 44.6 %, and a large detectivity (D*) of 3.5×1011 Jones at a bias voltage of 0 V and under 258 nm illumination with 0.72 μW light power. Compared to Pt/Lu2O3/GaN devices without embedded GQDs layers, this device has a higher R, a larger D*, and higher EQE. In addition, the application of the device in secure optical communication systems has verified the feasibility of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN in decrypting encrypted optical signals into readable text information. This work provides a new reference for the fabrication of high-performance DUV photovoltaic PDs based on Lu2O3.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Deep-ultraviolet (DUV) photodetectors (PDs) exhibit promising application prospects in secure communication, forest fire prevention, UV lithography technology, and other fields [1–3]. They can be broadly categorized into main types: photovoltaic and photoconductive two main types. Photovoltaic PDs are highly favored for their low power consumption and rapid response speed [4,5]. Currently, wide-bandgap semiconductor materials, represented by β-Ga2O3, AlN, and diamond, have been widely reported for the fabrication of DUV photovoltaic PDs [6–9]. Notably, lutetium oxide (Lu2O3), a novel wide-bandgap semiconductor, stands out with a bandgap of 5.5∼5.8 eV, robust thermal and chemical stability, and solution processability, making it an excellent candidate material for constructing DUV photovoltaic PDs [10–12].

DUV photovoltaic PDs based on Lu2O3 are typically fabricated by constructing heterojunctions and/or Schottky junctions [12–14]. In spite of the lack of external bias conditions, the separation and collection of photogenerated carriers can also be realized via the built-in electric field at the heterojunction interface and/or the Schottky junction interface. Due to lattice mismatch among different materials, there are bound to be multiple dangling bonds at the metal-semiconductor contact interface and the heterojunction interface [15,16]. The interface states associated with these dangling bonds are likely to become recombination centers for charge carriers, thus decreasing the lifetime and separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers [17,18]. This will weaken the photoyield of the DUV PD based on Lu2O3. It is obvious that the Lu2O3 DUV photovoltaic PD can be effectively enhanced by reducing the density of the interface states.

Low-dimensional materials, such as MXene, graphene, and Bi₂O₂Se, have attracted widespread attention in the field of high-performance optoelectronic device research due to their unique properties [19–22]. Among these material, zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that have fewer surface dangling bonds and high carrier mobility have been widely applied as defects passivation layer at the interface and ultrafast charge transport layer in photovoltaic devices [23–27]. Embedding GQDs into the metal/semiconductor interface as an interface defects passivation layer can improve the carrier separation and transport capability at the heterojunction interface, and effectively prolong the lifetime of carrier [28–30]. Moreover, the photo-induced pyroelectric effect of GQD can greatly enhance the charge collection and light capture efficiency [31]. Thus, this interface engineering modified with low-dimensional graphene, in theory, can effectively enhance the photoresponsivity of Lu2O3 DUV photovoltaic PD.

In this study, GQDs are embedded as modification layers at the Pt/Lu2O3 interface to construct a Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN DUV photovoltaic detector. The device, which exhibits an enhanced photo-induced pyroelectric and photovoltaic coupled effect, demonstrates a photoresponsivity (R) of up to 92.6 mA/W, a detectivity (D*) of 3.5×1011 Jones, and a high external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 44.6 % at 0 V bias and under 258 nm illumination with a light power of 0.72 μW. Meanwhile, the device also has a fast response speed of ∼45 ms. One reason for the high performance of the device is that the enhanced photo-induced pyroelectric effect of GQDs enhances the charge collection and light capture efficiency of the photodetector. Compared with the Pt/Lu2O3/GaN DUV PD without GQDs, the saturation photocurrent of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN has increased by 49 times, the D* has increased by 11 times, and the EQE has increased by 49 times at 0 V bias voltage. The reason for these improvements is that GQDs are added to the Pt/Lu2O3 contact interface as an interface passivation layer and a hole transport layer, which improves carrier separation efficiency, decreases carrier transfer time, and extends carrier lifetime. Furthermore, a secure optical communication system is constructed which consists of the transmitter and the receiver. The fabricated DUV PD converts the optical signal according to the International Morse Code to electrical signals and the function of decrypting an optical signal into a readable text message is achieved through the backward decryption of the electrical signal. It has been verified that the Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN DUV photovoltaic detector can be feasibly applied as the optical receiver of a secure optical communication system.

Fig. 1 displays the design concept and working principle of a Lu2O3 DUV PD with high EQE. In the case where Pt, GQDs, Lu2O3 and n-GaN do not come into contact with one another to form the Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction, the Fermi level positions of these materials are not aligned. Among them, the work function of Pt is 5.65 eV, the LUMO and HOMO of GQDs (p-type) are 3.74 eV and 6.01 eV, respectively [3,32]. The electron affinity (χ), bandgap (Eg ), and Fermi level position (EF : the distance between the Fermi level and the valence band maximum) of Lu2O3 are 2.0 eV, 5.8 eV, and 3.13 eV, respectively [13]. The corresponding values of the χ, Eg, and EF for GaN are 4.1 eV, 3.4 eV, and 3.3 eV, respectively [33]. The energy level matching of the GQDs, Lu2O3, and GaN shows that once GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction is formed, it is possible to efficiently separate the photogenerated carriers. Moreover, the GQDs layer embedded at the Pt/Lu2O3 interface not only acts as a defect passivation layer, but also a rapid transfer path for the interface holes, thereby increasing the separation efficiency and collection efficiency of photogenerated carriers in the device. Meanwhile, GQDs embedded on the surface of Lu2O3 will enhance the photo-induced pyroelectric effect. Compared to the polarization electric field at the Pt/Lu2O3 interface, a polarization electric field with higher intensity (the direction of which is consistent with the built-in electric field) will form at the GQDs/Lu2O3 interface. Then, the enhanced internal electric field supplied by GQDs/Lu2O3 interface results in an enhanced photovoltaic effect coupled with the enhanced pyroelectric effect due to the GQDs, which further increases the separation and collection efficiency of the photogenerated carriers under DUV irradiation and at zero-bias conditions. Accordingly, it is possible to fabricate Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN DUV photovoltaic detectors with high EQE according to the concept of aforementioned device design.

The preparation process for Lu2O3 films and Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN DUV PD devices is depicted in Fig. 2 a. Initially, a precursor solution containing 0.7 mol/L Lu (NO3)3 is coated on GaN substrate. This is followed by annealing at 600 °C for 1 hour to improve the crystallinity of the Lu2O3 film. Subsequently, GQDs (which are purchased from Suzhou TanFeng Graphene Technology Co., Ltd.) are spin-coated onto the prepared Lu2O3 films. Finally, platinum electrodes (positive electrode) with a thickness of ∼5 nm and indium electrodes (negative electrode) are deposited on GQDs/Lu2O3 and GaN substrates, respectively, to complete the construction of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN DUV PD.

According to the XRD pattern of Lu2O3/GaN (Fig. 2b), diffraction peaks corresponding to the (200), (211), (521), and (600) crystal planes of Lu2O3 can be observed, confirming that Lu2O3 film with polycrystal orientation is successfully deposited on a GaN substrate [10]. A comparison of Raman spectra of Lu2O3/GaN and GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN heterojunctions (Fig. 2c) is performed, and Raman peak centers at 1417 cm−1 and 1656 cm−1 (belonging to the D and G bands of GQDs) are observed in the GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction, indicating that GQD is coated on the surface of Lu2O3 films [34]. Furthermore, the Raman peaks associated with Lu2O3 are observed in the Raman spectra of the two heterojunctions, which further shows that the Lu2O3 films are successfully deposited on the GaN by solution method [35]. Meanwhile,the average size of the GQDs is about 9 nm, based on the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the GQDs (Fig. 2d). The thickness of the prepared Lu2O3 film is obtained to be ∼248 nm from the SEM image of GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction (Fig. 2d). The transmission spectrum of the Lu2O3 film deposited on the sapphire (prepared by the same solution method) shows a high transmissivity in DUV region (Fig. 2e). The band gap of the prepared Lu2O3 film is about 5.5 eV, according to the relation of photon energy and absorption coefficient (Fig. 2f) [36]. At the same time, it is found that the transmissivity of the electrode in the UV region is remarkably decreased by adding GQDs. This is observed by comparing the transmission spectra of the Pt electrode deposited on the sapphire and the Pt-GQDs electrode deposited on the sapphire, both of which adopted the same electrode deposition process (Fig. 2g). This can be attributed to the absorption of ultraviolet light from n → π* transition of CO bonds and some functional groups present on the surface of GQDs [37].

The photoelectric properties of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN devices are investigated. Fig. 3 a presents the I-V curves of the device under 258 nm light irradiation and dark state. The photocurrent is significantly higher than the dark current, indicating that the device is sensitive to DUV. Specifically, the device exhibits an open circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.52 V under DUV irradiation with opitcal power 9.4 μW, indicating that the device is a photovoltaic device with low power consumption characteristics. At the same time, the device also demonstrates a DUV (258 nm) photoresponsivity that is one order of magnitude higher than the UV (365 nm) responsivity at different bias conditions, suggesting that the device is more sensitive to DUV than to UV (Fig. 3b). From Fig. 3c, the linear I-V curve formed by the contact between the In electrode and GaN, as well as the nonlinear I-V curve formed by the contact between the Pt-GQDs electrode and the Lu2O3 film, reveal the ohmic contact characteristics of In electrode and the non-ohmic contact characteristics of Pt-GQDs electrode, which are likely due to the potential barrier between Pt-GQDs electrode and Lu2O3 film.

The I-T curve of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN under the condition of multi-cycle DUV irradiation with 9.4 μW shows that the device has a stability and repeatability of the DUV light detecting ability (Fig. 3d). The decay time (τd) of the photodetector is specified as the duration required for the photocurrent to decrease from 90 % of its saturation value to 10 % of that level after termination of DUV illumination. The rise time (τr) is defined as the duration required for the photocurrent to rise from 10 % to 90 % of its saturation value under illumination [38]. Thus, the τd and the τr of the detector can be estimated to be about 45 ms and 41 ms, respectively. The forward I-V curves of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN under DUV with variable optical power is presented in Fig. 3e, the VOC and the short-circuit current (ISC) of the device are gradually increased as the optical power of the DUV increases. Then, the variation of VOC and ISC with DUV optical power is investigated by fitting fitting the power function as follows [6]:
(1)
V
O C
= A
P
α
(2)
I
S C
= B
P
β
(Where P is the optical power, α and β are power exponents, and A and B are constants)

The power exponents obtained by the separate fitting are all not 1 (Fig. 3f and g), which means that the detector is in a supersaturated state. The PDCR of Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN PD is more than 10 at different optical power DUV illumiantion (Fig. 3h), which shows that the PD has an sensitive recognition of DUV.

According to the multi period I-T under variable optical power 258 nm illumination and 0 V bias voltage (Fig. 4 a), a peak photocurrent is generated at the moment of DUV exposure. As the optical power increases, the peak photocurrent of the device becomes more apparent. In this case, it is assumed that the peak photocurrent (defined as Iphoto+pry) is generated by the superposition of an current resulting from a photo-induced thermoelectric effect and a photovoltaic effect. The increase of the light intensity not only increases the photocurrent (Iphoto), but also increases the pyroelectric current (Ipry), thus improving the photoelectric performance of the device. The absence of the reverse peak current may be attributed to the formation time of the reverse polarization current being significantly shorter than the recombination time of photo-excited carriers in the device. Furthermore, COMSOL is used to simulate the heat transfer process of Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction in time, so as to explain how the pyroelectric-photovoltaic coupled effect can enhance the photoelectric performance of the device. A solid heat transfer model built into COMSOL is used to simulate the temperature of the prepared Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction. The geometric sizes of the Lu2O3 film are 1000 nm in width and 250 nm in depth; and for GaN, they are 1000 nm in width and 400 nm in depth. The initial temperature of the heterojunction is set to 298.15 K (room temperature). The mesh setting type is physical-field-control mesh, triangular mesh element, extremely refined.

Initially, there is no apparent temperature gradient in the Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction at the time of switching on the DUV with high-power-density (Fig. 4b). From Fig. 4b1, the difference in temperature between the top surface and the bottom surface of the Lu2O3 film is only 0.5 μK. Therefore, the photocurrent of devices without temperature gradient is only provided by the photovoltaic effect (Fig. 4b2). Subsequently, the Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction absorbs photon energy and a portion of the energy is absorbed in the form of phonons by the heterojunction. No significant temperature gradient is observed in the Lu2O3/GaN heterojunction after 1.5 seconds of continuous DUV irradiation (Fig. 4c). However, temperature difference between the top and bottom surfaces of lutetium oxide reaches 20.7 μK within a short period of 1 μs. The results show that a positive temperature gradient (ΔT > 0) may exist on the top and bottom surfaces of Lu2O3 films in a short time (Fig. 4c1). At this time, the photo-induced charge creates a polarization-built-in electric field (Epyro). Moreover, there is also an a built-in electric field (Ebuilt-in) in the same direction as the Epyro because of the Fermi level difference in materials. Thus, besides photocurrent (Iphoto), Ipyro current should also be taken into account in the overall output current of the device at the time of switching on the DUV.

From Fig. 5 a and b, it can be seen that the Iphoto+pyro and its corresponding photoresponsivity (Rphoto+pyro) of the device under 0 V bias and each DUV irradiation with different light power are larger than Iphoto and its corresponding photoresponsivity (Rphoto), respectively. The Rphoto+pyro of the device can reach 92.6 mA/W at 258 nm illumination of 0.72 μW. At the same time, the enhancement factor of current (ratio of Iphoto+pyro to Iphoto) and the enhancement factor of responsivity (ratio of Rphoto+pyro to Rphoto) both increase with the increase of optical power (Fig. 5c). This is due to the fact that a larger power DUV illumination results in a larger temperature fluctuation in the device, and thus a significant increase in Ipryo. This further indicates that the contribution of the photo-induced pyroelectric effect to the improvement of device performance cannot be neglected, particularly in the case of high-light-power irradiation. Meanwhile, the external quantum efficiency (EQE) and detectivity (D*) are important parameters for PDs. The EQE and D* of the devices under different light power DUV irradiation are further analyzed by the of pyroelectric and photovoltaic coupled effect and the single photovoltaic effect, respectivily. Based on the formula of EQE and D* [39]:
(3)
E Q E =
R hc
λ e
(4)
D
*
=
R
2 e
J
d a r k

(Where R is photoreponsivity of the device, h is Planck's constant, c is the speed of light, Jdark is the dark current density, λ is the wavelength of the incident light, and e is the elementary charge.)

It is calculated that under DUV illumination with a light power of 0.72 μW, the device has the maximum EQEphoto+pyro (44.6 %), which is 1.3 times that of EQEphoto (Fig. 5d). The 30 % increase in EQE is attributed to the contribution of the pyroelectric effect. Meanwhile, the maximum D*photo+pyro and D*photo are calculated to be 3.5×1011 Jones and 2.7×1011 Jones, respectively (Fig. 5e). Obviously, the enhanced photo-inducedpyroelectric and photovoltaic coupling effects provides a stronger polarization electric field and built-in electric field strength, which improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. This multi field coupling strategy provides a convenient approach for enhancing the performance of optoelectronic devices. In addition, the rise time and fall time of the device decrease with the increase of the optical power (Fig. 5f), which can be attributed to the increase in carrier recombination efficiency caused by the increase in the number of photo generated carriers.

Next, a detailed comparison is made between Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN (PD1) and Pt/Lu2O3/GaN (PD2) to assess the influence of the GQDs on the performance of the device. From Fig. 6 a and b, it can be seen that the current of PD1 is higher than that of PD2, either in the dark or under DUV illumination. The increase in the current is attributable to the high hole mobility of GQDs, which increases the transport rate of charge carriers at the metal/semiconductor interface. Meanwhile, the VOC of PD1 is slightly higher than that of PD2, suggesting that a higher carrier separation efficiency can be obtained in PD1. From the single cycle I-T curves of PD1 and PD2 under DUV irradiation and 0 V bias (Fig. 6c), the decay time of PD1 is longer than that of PD2. Although GQDs can serve as fast carrier channels to enhance the interface hole transport rate, they can also act as interface defect passivation layers, reducing the concentration of carrier recombination centers at the interface and extending the carrier lifetime. Therefore, PD1 and PD2 exhibit different response speeds, with PD1 showing a slower response speed than PD2.

Besides,the other photo-electric properties of PD1 and PD2 are described in detail in Fig. 6d and e. At a bias voltage of 0 V and 9.4 μW 258 nm illumination, PD1 exhibits a larger Rphoto (∼22.7 mA/W), D*photo (∼1.2×1011 Jones), larger photo-to- dark current ratio (PDCR∼146), and higher EQEphoto (∼11 %). Notably, Rphoto, D*photo and EQEphoto of PD1 are improved by more than one order of magnitude than that of PD2 (Fig. 6f). In other words, when two devices exhibit only a single photovoltaic effect, PD1′s performance is superior to that of PD2. This can be attributed to the reduction of carrier recombination center concentration at the interface due to the embedding of GQDs, which enhances the interface barrier height and thus improves the photogenerated carrier separation efficiency in PD1. All these results confirme that the GQDs play an important role in improving the performance of Lu2O3 based DUV PD.

The forward conduction threshold-voltage (Vth) of a diode is usually positively correlated with the built-in electric field strength of the diode. According to the positive I-V curves of PD1 and PD2 in the dark, the Vth values of PD1 and PD2 are 2.05 V and 1.54 V, respectively (Fig. 6g). This indicates that the strength of the built-in electric field at the Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3 interface is stronger, which makes it possible for PD1 to have a stronger photogenerated carrier separation capability. Furthermore, the I-V curve of the two devices in the dark is analyzed by the the thermionic emission model (Fig. 6h and i) [40]. Based on the thermionic emission model, the relationship between the voltage V and the potential barrier (φB ), and the relationship between the current density J and the V is as follows [40,41]:
(5)
V = R
A e
f f
J + n
ϕ B
+
k T
q
n ln
(
J
A
*
T
2
)
(6)
d ( V )
d ( ln J )
= R
A
e f f
J +
k T
q
n

(Where R is the series resistance of the device, n is the ideality factor, q is the electronic charge, V the voltage applied, k the Boltzmann constant, T the absolute temperature, A* = 120cm−2 K−2 A the Richardson constant, φB Schottky barrier height, Aeff the effective electrode area)

According to Eq. (6), it is possible to derive the ideal factors of PD1 and PD2 as 2.3 and 3.1, respectively. The ideal factor of PD1 is closer to 1 than that of PD2. The function H (J) is defined as follows:
(7)
H
( J )
= V −
k T
q
n ln
(
J
A
*
T
2
)

By substituting the calculated ideal factor into Eq. (5), and fitting H(J) and J by Eq. (7), it is possible to obtain the potential barrier height of PD1 and PD2 as 0.82 eV and 0.43 eV, respectively. The enhancement of the barrier height is attributed to the passivation effect of GQDs on the Pt/Lu2O3 interface defects.

Additionally, the performance of the Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN device is compared in detail with that of DUV photovoltaic PDs reported in recent years. From Table 1 , at 0 V bias conditions, the Pt GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN device exhibits a fast response speed, while demonstrating a ultra-high responsivity (R), an ultra-high EQE, and high detectivity (D*). The R and EQE of the device are one order of magnitude higher than those of most DUV photovoltaic PDs.

Finally, a secure optical communication system based on international Morse code encryption is constructed to verify the feasibility of PD1 for optical signal receivers in optical communication systems (Fig. 7 a). The secure optical communication system consists of a transmitter (TX port) and a receiver (RX port). Among them, the signal of the DUV photodiode is compiled by Keithley 2400, sent to PD1, and then output by Keithley 4200A-SCS. After the long pulse is defined as "dash" and the short pulse as "dot" (Fig. 7b), it is possible to derive the information contained in the light signal by decoding the electric signal output from PD1. From Fig. 7c, the encrypted optical signal is successfully decoded into "Hello world" text message, indicating that the Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN device is capable of being used as an optical receiver in a secure communication system.

In summary, a high-performance Pt-GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN DUV photovoltaic PD has been successfully developed, featuring a responsivity of 92.6 mA/W, an EQE of 44.6 %, and a D* of up to 3.5 × 1011 Jones under 0 V bias. Embedding GQDs at Pt/Lu2O3 interface has led to a significant improvement in EQE for the device. The significantly enhanced performance of the device is attributed to the interface embedded GQDs, which not only enhance the photo-induced thermoelectric and photovoltaic coupling effect but also decrease the Pt/Lu2O3 interface density and improve carrier transport capability at the interface. The proposed interface engineering approach provides new design ideas for constructing heterojunction DUV PDs, demonstrating its enormous potential in improving PD performance. In addition, optical communication applications based on Pt/GQDs/Lu2O3/GaN devices have shown broad prospects. This detector can effectively decrypt encrypted optical signals and convert them into readable text information, demonstrating the important role of DUV PD in secure optical communication systems. This research achievement not only provides new possibilities for the application of optical communication and security protection but also lays the foundation for future exploration in fields such as environmental monitoring and medical diagnosis.

Dan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Weisen Li: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiarong Liang: Visualization, Software, Resources. Zhuogeng Lin: Methodology, Data curation. Xingui Tang: Resources. Wei Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by by the open research fund of Songshan Lake Materials Laboratory (No. 2023SLABFK01), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Gamma-Gamma Collider and Its Comprehensiv Applications (2024KSYS001), the Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (No. JCYJ20240813151434045) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62004229).
